text.fullText: From: hansen1167@everyactioncustom.com To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/9/2026 4:32:42 PM Subject: Docket ID# EPA–HQ–OW–2025–2929 — Opposition to Proposed 2026 Changes to Circumscribe Certification Authority of States and Tribes -------------------------------------------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------- [You don't often get email from hansen1167@everyactioncustom.com. Learn why this is important at https://aka.ms/LearnAboutSenderIdentification ] Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. Dear Administrator Lee Zeldin, I am writing this afternoon in strong opposition to the Environment Protections Agency's proposed 2026 rule changes which seek to circumscribe certification authority used by States and Tribes to protect clean water. The EPA is the very entity that reported that water resource stewardship done by Tribal Nations has a higher bar and exceeds the EPAs standards. (Ending the Interminable Gap in Indian Country Water Quality Protection,” Harvard Environmental Law Review, Vol. 45, No. 1, 2020-21| Author: James Grijalva.) We should not take away Tribal water sovereignty or right to be treated as a state. We all benefit from the economic benefits of Tribal stewarded and rehabilitated land, and the public and private money that follows these projects as local investments. It also helps to eliminate the high cost of pollution mitigation and reduce disruption to household water supplies, whether city water or a well. We should be transferring more land to Tribal Nations to be stewarded, not less. The Clean Water Act was adopted by Congress to restore and maintain clean water and to ensure that States and Tribes could prevent violation of the water quality requirements they have adopted. The proposed rule change goes against the common good. The EPA’s proposed certification rules would serve the special interests of polluters at the expense of clean water, the rights of States to protect the health and welfare of their residents, and the sovereignty of Tribes. The 2026 certification rules proposed by the EPA are unlawful and unreasonable. My opposition is specifically regarding... 1. ) The Rule’s elimination of current regulations facilitating Tribal certification authority and supporting neighboring/downstream Tribes in objecting to federal permits and preventing violations of Reservation water quality requirements. Shame on you for erasing Tribes from these regulations. 2. ) I oppose the Rule’s restrictions that would prevent any State or Tribe in the United States from setting conditions on Federal “general permits.” In Minnesota, we’ve used existing certification authority to prevent degradation and protect streams, Exceptional Aquatic Life Use waters, and Outstanding Resource Value waters, like the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness and Voyageurs National Park. 3. ) I am opposed to all the Rule’s changes that tighten a noose around the scope of certification. They would prevent conditions or denial of Federal permits even if activities would violate State and Tribal standards and contaminate lakes and streams due to wetlands destruction, waste stockpiling, or seepage through groundwater into surface water. 4. ) I'm opposed to the Rule’s forced waiver of State or Tribal certification after a year even if time is needed due to public process, acts of God, or because the project proposer concealed the truth about water impacts in its draft permit application. This rule would encourage polluters to hide the facts and do a bait-and-switch after time has run out for certification. 5. ) I oppose the Rule’s creation of new power in the EPA Administrator to determine without guardrails that an entire category of discharges will never affect downstream States or Tribes and deprive them of authority to object to violations of water quality requirements. What category would this EPA remove from certification review? All pipeline discharge? All mining waste? Please, I urge you, the EPA must reject or withdraw its 2026 proposed rule to undermine State and Tribal certification. Existing rules under the Clean Water Act do a better job of protecting all of us from contaminated water, whether for drinking, swimming, or fishing. Thank you for your work and for consideration of my comments. Sincerely, Jaden Hansen 2141 Minneapolis Ave Minneapolis, MN 55406-4239 hansen1167@gmail.com
